title.emphasis: Section I: The Legal Instrument as Copyrightable Expression

section.para: The foundation of any security is its legal definition—the specific text that establishes its rights, limitations, and obligations. This body of text, as an "original work of authorship," is the first and most fundamental layer of intellectual property protection.1

title.emphasis: 1.1 The Security's DNA: Articles, Bylaws, and Agreements

section.para: The legal form of a cooperative security is not a single document but a collection of interrelated legal instruments that, together, constitute a copyrightable work. Copyright protection arises automatically upon the creation of these documents in a fixed form, though formal registration with the U.S. Copyright Office is essential for robust enforcement.1
The key copyrightable documents that define the legal instrument include:

listitem.para: Articles of Incorporation: This foundational document, filed with the state, establishes the cooperative as a legal entity and can set forth the basic capital structure, including the authorization of different classes of shares (e.g., common and preferred).4

Bylaws: This is where the core principles of the cooperative are implemented in specific, legally binding language. The bylaws detail membership requirements, democratic governance procedures (e.g., one-member, one-vote), the mechanics of patronage distribution, and, crucially, any restrictions on the transfer of shares—all of which define the unique character of the cooperative security.7

Offering Memorandum & Subscription Agreement: For any security offered to investors, these documents are paramount. The offering memorandum discloses the terms, conditions, and risks of the investment, while the subscription agreement is the specific contract executed between the cooperative and the investor.9 The precise language in these documents—defining dividend rights, redemption policies, and voting limitations for a new class of preferred stock, for example—is a protectable creative expression.9

section.para: For a cooperative inventing a new class of security, copyrighting the legal architecture is a critical defensive measure. It prevents a competitor from simply copying the exact contractual language and structure that may have taken considerable legal and financial expertise to develop. For instance, the specific legal clauses that create a novel "community investor" share class, balancing a limited financial return with restricted voting rights to preserve member control, are protectable expressions.15 An attempt by a hostile corporation to replicate this model by duplicating the bylaws and offering documents would constitute copyright infringement.

Therefore, the first step in securing a new cooperative instrument is to treat its foundational legal documents as valuable intellectual property and secure their copyright through formal registration.1
